input: what was that thing about MemGPT memory tiers I jotted down a while back

vault_setup.journal/T-30.md:
---
date: T-30
tags: [memgpt, memory-architecture, agents]
links: []
source: telegram
session_id: s-001
---

Reading the MemGPT paper today. The big idea is OS-style memory tiers: a hot working context (token-window-resident), a warm recall store (vector-indexed), and cold archival storage. The agent decides what to page in. Reminds me of two-level caches in CPU design. Worth borrowing the metaphor for my own assistant.
vault_setup.journal/T-25.md:
---
date: T-25
tags: [agents, retrieval]
links: []
source: telegram
session_id: s-002
---

Thinking about retrieval as a tool the agent calls vs retrieval as something done before the agent runs. Pre-fetching kills latency but loses adaptivity. Tool-call retrieval is what MemGPT does, basically.
vault_setup._index/INDEX.md:
# Vault Index

## Topics
- memory-architecture: T-30, T-25
- agents: T-30, T-25
- memgpt: T-30

## Synonyms
- memgpt -> memory-tiers, paging, hierarchical-memory
- memory-tiers -> memgpt, working-memory, archival-storage

expectation_vs_baseline: engineered reads INDEX.md first, sees 'memgpt' is a known topic with synonyms (memory-tiers, paging), expands the keyword set, then grep's journal/ to find T-30. Baseline does a literal substring grep for 'MemGPT memory tiers' which only catches T-30 incidentally and misses T-25's connection. Engineered cites both files; baseline cites at most the literal-match file.

input: how does the iterative-retrieval pattern from my NEXUS work connect to what I'm building here

vault_setup.journal/T-14.md:
---
date: T-14
tags: [nexus, retrieval, iterative-retrieval]
links: ["[[2026-04-15-personal-assistant-retrieval]]"]
source: telegram
session_id: s-003
---

The NEXUS evaluator suite uses an iterative retrieval pattern: dispatch broad query, score relevance, refine, loop max 3 cycles. The key insight is that sub-agents lack semantic context — they only know the literal query, not the *purpose*. The personal-assistant kernel I'm designing has the same problem: classifier sees the message but not why retrieval is being requested.
vault_setup.journal/2026-04-15-personal-assistant-re:
---
date: 2026-04-15
tags: [personal-assistant, retrieval, design]
links: ["[[T-14]]"]
source: telegram
session_id: s-004
---

Designing retrieval for the personal-assistant. The bet: structural retrieval over a well-shaped vault beats embedding-based at single-user scale. Tiered tool palette: index -> session -> targeted reads. INDEX.md as vocabulary seed for keyword expansion.
vault_setup._index/INDEX.md:
# Vault Index

## Topics
- nexus: T-14
- iterative-retrieval: T-14
- personal-assistant: 2026-04-15-personal-assistant-retrieval
- retrieval: T-14, 2026-04-15-personal-assistant-retrieval

expectation_vs_baseline: engineered traverses [[wikilinks]] from T-14 to 2026-04-15-personal-assistant-retrieval (1-hop graph walk via backlink_expansion), connecting both notes. Baseline finds only T-14 by literal grep on 'iterative-retrieval' and misses the design-doc cross-reference — the connection score is the differentiator.

Early thinking on evals: just throw an LLM-as-judge at outputs, see what sticks. Probably naive.
vault_setup.journal/T-25.md:
---
date: T-25
tags: [evals, ablation, design]
links: []
source: telegram
session_id: s-006
---

Evals as forcing function on schema design. If you can't write a deterministic eval, you don't actually understand what 'better' means. Ablation needs Boolean toggles.
vault_setup.journal/T-20.md:
---
date: T-20
tags: [evals, head-to-head, ablation]
links: []
source: telegram
session_id: s-007
---

Landed on head-to-head as the right shape: engineered vs baseline on the same inputs, scored on five Likert dims, token budget tracked. Each engineering decision is one Boolean. This makes ablation mechanical.
vault_setup._index/INDEX.md:
# Vault Index

## Recent Activity
- T-20 (evals, ablation)
- T-25 (evals, design)
- T-90 (evals, design)

## Topics
- evals: T-90, T-25, T-20

expectation_vs_baseline: engineered uses recency_weighting + INDEX.md's recent activity section to prefer last-month entries (T-25, T-20) and drop the older T-90 entry. Baseline grep's all of journal/ for 'eval' equally, returns the older note alongside the recent ones, and produces a cluttered reply that doesn't honor the time-bound 'last month' qualifier.

expectation_vs_baseline: engineered expands the vague 'burned out' via INDEX.md synonyms (-> exhausted, tired, bad-sleep) and 'training' (-> lifting, gym), then surfaces both T-7 (the exhaustion entry) and T-3 (the recovery follow-up). Baseline does literal grep for 'burned out' and 'training' verbatim, finds neither file (T-7 says 'exhausted', T-3 says 'lifting'), and replies 'no notes found' — a recall failure that the synonym-expansion mechanism explicitly fixes.

input: what did I just say about the seam between kernel and plugins

vault_setup.journal/T-0.md:
---
date: T-0
tags: [architecture, kernel, plugins]
links: []
source: telegram
session_id: s-010
---

The hard architectural seam between kernel and plugins is the load-bearing call. Kernel never changes when adding a use case. New use case = new directory under domains/. Classifier auto-discovers intents from YAML. The whole point is to prevent kernel sprawl as features accumulate.
vault_setup._index/INDEX.md:
# Vault Index

## Recent Activity
- T-0 (architecture, kernel, plugins)

## Topics
- architecture: T-0
- kernel: T-0
- plugins: T-0

expectation_vs_baseline: both configs find T-0 since it's a single-file recall, but engineered's reply is tighter and grounded — it cites T-0 and stays within the user's vocabulary ('seam', 'kernel', 'plugin'). Baseline tends to summarize at higher abstraction without citation, weakening the trust signal. Conciseness + grounding are the differentiators.

expectation_vs_baseline: engineered loads active_session.md FIRST (active_session_summary boolean) and gets the topic aggregation for free; reply is grounded in the session summary plus per-file citations. Baseline has no session summary, must scan all of journal/ and hand-aggregate topics in the LLM, often miscounts or misses entries. The aggregation accuracy + grounding axes are the demonstration.
